authoritative_sources.title: Application and Instructions
authoritative_sources.publisher: City of Seattle Special Events Office
authoritative_sources.source_class: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Seattle Special Events Office is an official municipal permitting authority. Its application instructions define the artifact set event organizers must provide, including event maps, run-of-show details, vendor lists, insurance documents, and public-safety-plan information.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: accepted intake, application completeness review, artifact collection, and pre-event readiness packaging

authoritative_sources.title: Permitting Process
authoritative_sources.publisher: City of Seattle Special Events Office
authoritative_sources.source_class: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official city permitting workflow explains when a special-event permit is required, the application timeline, fee-payment gating, neighborhood-notice timing, and the relationship between permit issuance and required approvals.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: permit qualification, application timing, permit issuance gating, and post-event fee adjustment handling

authoritative_sources.title: Insurance
authoritative_sources.publisher: City of Seattle Special Events Office
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official municipal risk-management guidance specifies insurance artifacts, minimum coverage, additional-insured requirements, alcohol-liability conditions, and states that the permit will not be issued until insurance is approved.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: risk review, permit release gating, alcohol-sensitive event handling, and permit revocation sensitivity
used_for: refusal to progress without approved coverage

authoritative_sources.title: Public Safety and Event Management
authoritative_sources.publisher: City of Seattle Special Events Office
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official city public-safety guidance ties event safety, crowd management, emergency plans, and staffing thresholds to Seattle Fire Code requirements and defines when public safety plans, crowd managers, police staffing, and medical staffing become controlling prerequisites.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: safety readiness, event-day crowd management, incident escalation, and emergency-plan execution
used_for: event-day staffing and crowd-density blocker logic

authoritative_sources.title: Federal Trade Commission Announces Bipartisan Rule Banning Junk Ticket and Hotel Fees
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC is the primary U.S. federal consumer-protection enforcer. This official rule announcement is directly relevant to live-event ticketing because it requires up-front total-price disclosure, prohibits misleading fee presentation, and states that the rule took effect on May 12, 2025.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: guest-facing pricing, ticket-offer presentation, checkout disclosure, and pricing-related exception handling
used_for: refusal to improvise or defend nontransparent pricing
guest-service escalation when pricing policy determines the answer

authoritative_sources.title: How to fix mistakes in your credit card bill
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CFPB is the primary U.S. consumer-finance regulator for card billing-error handling. Its guidance establishes written-notice timing, investigation periods, and issuer-versus-merchant dispute boundaries relevant to duplicate ticket charges, missing refunds, and payment mismatches.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: payment mismatch review, refund-not-posted handling, and merchant-versus-issuer routing
used_for: required evidence for duplicate-charge and refund disputes

authoritative_sources.title: Better Online Ticket Sales Act
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FTC's BOTS Act page is the official federal statement of the anti-circumvention baseline for online event ticketing and is directly relevant to suspicious access-control circumvention, bot activity, and resale-sensitive ticket cases.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ticket-fraud triage, suspicious access handling, and escalation to fraud or loss-prevention owners

authoritative_sources.title: Standards and Guidance
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Event Safety Alliance is a recognized event-safety body whose public standards-and-guidance page identifies the Event Safety Guide and ANSI ES1 standards for communications, weather preparedness, crowd management, event security, fire safety, and temporary special event structures.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: event-specific terminology normalization, safety planning, weather and communication readiness, and temporary-structure awareness
used_for: crowd, communication, weather, security, and structure-aware readiness

workflow_stages.name: accepted intake and governing-record verification
workflow_stages.description: Confirm venue, organizer, event category, jurisdiction, permit requirement, current event-map revision, run-of-show version, and the systems of record that make the request safe to process.
source_classes: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: permit, insurance, and public-safety readiness gating
workflow_stages.description: Validate permit status, fee-payment posture, insurance approval, vendor and staffing prerequisites, and public-safety-plan completeness before the event may move into active execution.
source_classes: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance

workflow_stages.name: event-day ticketing, guest, vendor, and throughput execution
workflow_stages.description: Track guest ingress, ticket scans, will-call exceptions, vendor arrivals, staffing coverage, run-of-show progression, and queue pressure while preserving record-of-truth links.
source_classes: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references

workflow_stages.name: exception handling for pricing, refunds, safety, and suspicious access
workflow_stages.description: Package ticketing mismatches, refund exceptions, crowd-density risks, emergency conditions, and suspicious ticket or payment activity without absorbing pricing, fraud, or safety authority.
source_classes: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance

workflow_stages.name: wrap, settlement review, and completion evidence
workflow_stages.description: Close the event only when strike, settlement, incident review, refund-queue review, and post-event records are complete enough to support an audit-ready completion record.
source_classes: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references

artifacts: permit applications, event categories, permit terms, fee invoices, and permit-release records
event maps, site layouts, ingress and egress plans, barricade plans, run-of-show timelines, and load-in or load-out schedules
ticketing manifests, order identifiers, seat or section inventory, guest lists, credential rosters, will-call records, POS settlements, refund queues, and promotion records
vendor and supplier lists, certificates of insurance, additional-insured endorsements, alcohol or noise permits, and staffing assignments
public-safety plans, crowd-manager assignments, emergency contacts, monitor posting plans, medical and fire staffing notes, weather contingencies, and incident logs
wrap reports, strike completion notes, settlement reconciliations, refund reviews, and closure approvals
systems_of_record: venue booking, CRM, or PMS system
order management and guest-service desk system
permit portal or municipal permitting repository
document-control repository for event maps, run of show, insurance, and vendor files

allowed: classify event-production work as in-scope, out-of-scope, blocked, ready, active, wrap-pending, rework-required, or complete based on documented execution state and evidence
request or validate the minimum tenant facts, permit records, ticketing records, payment records, safety-plan records, and systems-of-record evidence needed to continue safely
prepare evidence-backed status updates, completion records, and escalation packages without overstating pricing, refund, fraud, or safety authority
escalate: price, fee, promotion, capacity, complimentary-inventory, or refund outcomes that require pricing or guest-policy approval
counterfeit-ticket, resale, bot, chargeback, or suspicious-access activity requiring fraud or loss-prevention ownership
crowd-density, ingress and egress, fire, medical, police, security, severe-weather, or emergency-command decisions
permit overrides, insurance sufficiency judgments, legal interpretation, or executive event-cancellation authority
refuse: mark an event ready to proceed when permit, insurance, public-safety-plan, staffing, or ticketing evidence is missing and would change the outcome
mark the event complete without strike, settlement, incident, and refund-queue evidence
treat live-event production as generic hospitality support when permit, crowd-management, ticketing, or safety controls materially change what can proceed

domain_failure_modes: an event proceeds without the governing permit, insurance, or public-safety plan because readiness was inferred from schedule pressure instead of authoritative records
guest entry, ticket scans, and payment settlement drift apart and the lane closes work against the wrong source of truth
an out-of-policy refund, fee waiver, or inventory release is promised even though pricing or guest-services approval is required
suspicious duplicate tickets, resale abuse, or chargeback-sensitive activity is treated as ordinary guest friction and not escalated to fraud or loss-prevention
crowd-density, ingress and egress, fire-watch, medical, or weather concerns are handled like routine throughput issues instead of safety blockers
completion is declared without strike confirmation, settlement review, incident review, or refund-queue review, making closure non-auditable
the lane drifts into pricing, refund, fraud, safety, permit, or executive authority because the event is already in execution

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Refund or policy-exception request outside lane authority
Customer-impact issue with safety or fraud implications
Execution exception that requires rework or escalation
Request to mark work complete without evidence of completion

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific permit thresholds, notification timing, and emergency-plan criteria vary materially across cities, parks, campuses, and private venues.
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: As of April 10, 2026, Seattle provides a concrete public baseline for permit workflow, but other jurisdictions can change which event features require permits, plans, notice, or staffing. Runtime must retrieve the governing venue and permit authority before concluding that a specific event may proceed.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Ticketing-platform, venue, and promoter contracts materially change who owns refunds, fees, settlement timing, and guest communication.
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: FTC and CFPB sources provide the public baseline for price disclosure and payment disputes, but tenant contracts still determine whether the venue, promoter, ticketing platform, or card issuer is the correct next owner for a particular refund or dispute.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant event models such as season passes, memberships, comp allocations, campground or resort packages, and mixed POS/PMS checkouts change artifact shape without replacing the public baseline.
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Runtime must supply the tenant-specific access model, booking flow, and record-of-truth mapping before the lane finalizes decisions involving bundled access, season-pass scans, or nonstandard settlement records.

notes: Authoritative records for this lane are the current permit and fee posture, approved event-map and run-of-show revision, ticketing and payment records, insurance and public-safety evidence, and the wrap or incident package designated by the tenant.
The lane stops before pricing overrides, refund exceptions, fraud findings, permit waivers, police or fire command, or final safety signoff even when guest pressure or show-time pressure is acute.
